優化機器人移動式倉儲揀貨系統中之庫存單位
分配：透過訂單頻率、產品週轉率、關聯性與
尺寸限制最大化貨架使用效率 
Optimizing SKU Allocation in RMFS Warehouses: 
Maximizing Pod Utilization Through Order 
Frequency, Product Turnover, Correlation, and 
Dimensional Constraints 
 
研 究 生： Fransisca Cindy Mulyani 
指導教授： 周碩彥 博士 
 郭伯勳 博士 
 
 
中華民國 114 年 07 月





 
 
i 
摘要 
機器人移動式倉儲系統已成為滿足現代電子商務倉儲效率需求的變革性解
決方案。然而，SKU 與儲位的最佳化分配在考慮 SKU 差異性與實際儲存限制等
現實營運條件時，仍是一項關鍵挑戰。 
本研究提出一套結構化、數據驅動的  SKU 分配策略，旨在在 RMFS 環境
中優化儲位空間利用率，同時提升訂單吞吐量與  Pile-On 表現。該方法將需求頻
率、產品週轉率、商品關聯性及尺寸可行性整合為一套統一的分類與配置策略。
首先，透過 K-means 分群法依據需求頻率對 SKU 進行分類，再以變異係數依需求
穩定性進一步細分。這些分類策略分別應用於三種配置情境：隨機分配、單倉單
類、單倉混類。為進一步提升可存取性，本研究引入分散策略，將高需求 SKU 分
散至多個儲位。研究透過 NetLogo 建立模擬模型，並結合 Python 最佳化模組，在
尺寸限制條件下及加入分散策略後評估各情境的效能表現。 
結果顯示，在尺寸限制條件下，「單倉混類」策略表現最佳，訂單吞吐率
達 91.90%，Pile-On 為 9.79，其次為「單倉單類」與「隨機分配」。引入分散策
略後，各情境的兩項效能指標均有進一步提 升，其中「單倉混類」策略的吞吐率
提升至 99.22%，Pile-On 提升至 9.96。ANOVA 檢定結果顯示，在尺寸限制及分散
策略下，三種情境在吞吐率與 Pile-On 上的差異均具有統計顯著性。成對 t 檢定進
一步驗證，分散策略對所有情境的兩項效能指標均帶來統計上顯著的改善。研究
結果證明，將基於需求的分類方法與分散策略及尺寸可行性結合，能夠在  RMFS 
環境中實現更有效的 SKU 與儲位分配。 
關鍵詞：機器人移動式倉儲系統、SKU 分配、儲位分配、變異係數、尺寸限制、
混合策略、分散策略、訂單吞吐量、Pile-On。  

 
 
ii 
ABSTRACT 
 
Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems have emerged as a transformative solution 
to meet the efficiency demands of modern e-commerce warehouses. Yet, the optimization 
of SKU-to-pod assignment remains a critical challenge, especially when considering real-
world operational constraints such as SKU variability and physical storage limitations.  
This research proposes a structured, data -driven SKU allocation policy aimed at 
optimizing pod space utilization while impro

ving throughput and pile-on performance in 
RMFS environments. The methodology integrates demand frequency, product turnover, 
item correlation, and dimensional feasibility into a unified classification and placement 
strategy. SKUs are first categorized by demand frequency using K-means clustering, and 
further class

ified based on demand stability using the Coefficient of Variation.  These 
classifications are applied under three allocation configurations: Random placement, One 
Pod One Class, and One Pod Mixed Class. To further enhance accessibility, a dispersion 
policy is introduced to distribute SKUs across multiple pods . A simulation model 
developed in NetLogo, combined with optimization modules in Python, evaluates 
perfo

rmance under dimensional constraints and with the dispersion policy applied. 
Results in dicate that under dimensional constraints, the One Pod Mixed Class 
strategy achieved the highest performance, with throughput reaching 91.90% and pile-on 
9.79, followed by One Pod One Class and the Random baseline. The introduction of the 
dispersion policy  further improved both metrics across all scenarios, with throughput 
increasing to 99.22% and pile -on to 9.96  for Mixed Class . Statistical validation using 
ANOVA confirmed significant differences in throughput and pile -on among the three 
scenarios under both dimensional constraints and with the dispersion policy. Paired t-tests 
further verified that disp

ersion produced statistically significant improvements in both 
metrics for all s cenarios. These results demonstrate that combining demand -based 
classification with dispersion and dimensional feasibility leads to more effective SKU -
to-pod assignments in RMFS environments. 
 
 
Keywords: Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System, SKU Allocation, Storage Assignment, 
Coefficient of Variation, Dimensional Constraints, Mixture, Dispersion, Order 
Throughput, Pile-On.

CHAPTER 1 
INTRODUCTION 
1.1 Background and Motivation 
The e -commerce sector has witnessed exponential growth in rec

ent years, 
significantly reshaping global retail operations and consumer purchasing behaviors. 
As of 2024, the global logistics market for e-commerce is estimated at approximately 
USD 500.76 billion, with projections estimating a rapid increase to USD 609.59 
billion in 2025, and exceeding USD 3.7 trillion by 2034, reflecting a compound 
annual growth rate (CAGR) of 22.3% [1]. This surge of expansion is primarily driven 
by the rising demand for fast, accurate, and technology-supported logistics services, 
especially in the B2C segment. The evolution of this market is illustrated in Figure 
1.1, which shows the projected regional growth of e -commerce logistics from 202 0 
to 2034, highlighting the dominant role of North America and the fast -growing 
influence of Asia Pacific. 
 
Figure 1. 1 E-Commerce Logistics Market Size by Region [1] 
In addition to major e -commerce players like Amazon, a growing number of  
department stores and supermarkets have also provided online shopping platforms. 
This widespread shift has significantly expanded the scale of distribution centers and 
delivery operatio

ns [2].  
This rapid rise of e-commerce has been significantly accelerated by the COVID -
19 pandemic, which acted as a major catalyst for digital transformation in the retail 
sector. Widespread lockdowns and social distancing policies compelled many bric k 


 
 
2 
and mortar retailers to temporarily shut down physical stores, prompting an urgent 
shift toward digital commerce and technology-driven f

ulfillment solutions. This shift 
led to the rapid adoption of systems such as online ordering, click-and-collect services, 
and robotics -assisted operations to maintain continuity in order fulfillment under 
restrictive conditions [3], [4].  
Prior to the pandemic, traditional e-commerce fulfillment relied heavily on manual 
labor for handling and processing orders [5]. However, as demand surged and 
efficiency requirements grew more stringent, many warehouses began transitioning 
toward automation [6]. Recent advancements in robotics have enabled the emergence 
of hybrid fulfillment models that combine human labor with autonomous agents, 
improving accuracy, scalab

ility, and responsiveness [7], [8].  
This transition toward automation has fundamentally transformed the logistics and 
warehousing landscape, but it has also introduced new operational challenges. 
Modern e-commerce warehouses must now cope with a large assortment of SKUs, 
frequent small-batch orders, strict delivery windows, and highly variable workloads 
throughout the day [9]. One of the most labor-intensive and costly operations within 
a warehouse is order fulfillment, particularly the order picking process , the retrieval 
of items to complete customer orders which accounts for 55% of the total warehouse 
operating expenses [10], [11], [12] . Moreover, o nly about 40% of direct labor 
activities are devoted to  productive tasks like loading, unloading, stocking, and 
picking, whereas the remaining 60% is primarily spent on unproductive travel within 
the warehouse [13]. Due to these rising  complexities of e -commerce orders, 
traditional manual picking systems struggle to maintain efficiency. As a result, many 
warehouses have started integrating automation technologies, such as Robotic Mobile 
Fulfillment Systems, to optimize operations. 
Wurman et al.

[14] introduced a pioneering approach to warehouse automation, 
proposing the use of autonomous mobile robots to transport storage units, later termed 
“pods”, to designated locations known as “workstations,” where human operators 
complete the picking process. This innovative system, which became widely known 
as the Kiva system, laid the foundation for a new generation of robotic fulfillment 
solutions [15], [16]. Since its initial deployment by Kiva systems in 2012  [17], the 
Robotic Mobile Fulf illment System has gained widespread adoption in global e -
commerce warehouses. Leading companies such as Amazon, Walgreens, Staples, 

 
 
3 
JD.com, and Alibaba’s Cainiao Network have integrated RMFS into their order 
fulfillment operations to enhance efficiency and scalability [18]. 
 
Figure 1. 2 Shelves in Amazon Warehouse [19] 
 
Figure 1. 3 Workflow in RMFS Warehouse [20] 
The components within RMFS are highly interdependent, creating a complex 
operational environment. While the system offers massive improvements over 
traditional warehousing methods, it also introduces a range of decision -making

problems. Research on RMFS ha s predominantly focused on addressing various 
optimization, assignment, and scheduling problems to enhance overall system 
performance [21]. 
While the optimization of RMFS has been extensively investigated by scholars in 
recent years, research on categorized storage

remains limited. Current studies 
primarily focus on improving robot routing, order sequencing, and pod retrieval 


 
 
4 
strategies, yet t he fundamental problem of efficient SKU allocation to storage 
locations has not received the same level of attention. In RMFS warehouses, the way 
SKUs are assigned to pods significantly impacts retrieval efficiency, system 
throughput, and overall warehouse  performance [2]. A poorly optimized SKU 
allocation strategy may lead to excessive pod retrievals , higher energy consumption 
and longer order fulfillment time. 
Another critical issue in SKU-to-pod assignment is pile-on efficiency, which refers 
to the number of items that can be picked from a single pod visit. In an optimized 
RMFS, frequently co -ordered SKUs should be stored together in the same pod to 
reduce the number of pod retrievals per order [22]. Poor SKU placement disrupts this 
balance, forcing robots to retrieve multiple pods for a single order. Consequently, 
retrieval delays increase, fulfillment speeds decline, and

warehouse throughput is 
negatively affected. 
In light of these challenges, this research specifically investigates the issue of SKU 
allocation in RMFS warehouses, aiming to assign SKUs to pods in a way that 
maximizes order throughput and pile -on. By leveraging historical order data , 
specifically SKU order frequency , order quantity and dimensional constraints, this 
study seeks to develop a storage assignment optimization model that enhances picking 
efficiency and operational performance. 
 
1.2 Objective 
The primary objective of this research is to develop an effective SKU allocation 
strategy for Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) t hat enhances storage 
efficiency and order fulfillment performance. Specifically, this study aims to: 
1. Increase order throughput by designing a SKU-to-pod assignment approach that 
reduces pod retrieval delays and supports continuous picking operations. 
2. Improve pile-on efficiency by grouping frequently co-ordered SKUs within the 
same pod to maximize the number of items that can be picked from a single pod 
visit. 
3.

Enhance space utilization by applying a categorized storage method that groups 
SKUs systematically based on order frequency, demand stability, and physical 
compatibility. 

 
 
5 
By achieving these goals, this study contributes to the advancement of storage 
allocation strategies in RMFS environments. It addresses the current research gap in 
categorized SKU placement and provides practical insights for warehouse managers 
aiming to improve space usage, fulfillment speed, and overall system efficiency in 
autom

ated warehouses. 
 
1.3 Scope and Limitations  
This study is conducted within a defined scope and under specific assumptions, 
described as follows: 
• The primary focus in this study is on small order sizes with multi-item orders 
typical of e-commerce fulfillment. Large bulk orders are excluded from this 
study and are assumed to be handled at sep

arate warehouse facilities. 
• This study focuses exclusively on the SKU-to-pod assignment policy within 
warehouse operations. Other key processes, such as order -to-station 
assignment (POA – Pick Order Assignment) and pod -to-station assignment 
(PPS – Pick Pod Selection), dynamic replenishment, and robot routing are 
handled using brute-force methods rather than optimized algorithms. 
• The data used for simulation is ad apted from actual cases, with the study 
modeling a total of 7,024 unique SKUs to ref

lect a realistically large SKU 
range. 
• All SKUs, including those with low demand, are assumed to be stored in the 
warehouse. Each pod is designed with multiple compartments,  and each 
compartment can contain only a single type of SKU. 
• The Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) modeled in this study are assumed 
to

perform both picking and replenishment tasks . However, the assignment 
of of AMRs to specialized task are beyond the scope of this work. 
Additionally, this study does not address the battery charging or management 
aspects of AMR operations. 
• It is assumed that the replenishment station always maintains adequate 
inventory levels to meet replenishment demand,  ensuring that SKUs are 
always available when required during pod replenishment process. 
• The warehouse environment is modeled using an Agent -Based Modeling 
(ABM) approach implemented in NetLogo. The simulation integrates Python 

 
 
6 
for optimization tasks an

d u ses Excel for data management and storage  of 
simulation results. 
 
1.4 Organization of Thesis 
This study is organized into five chapters, each addressing a specific aspect of the 
research process. The organization of the study are as follows: 
Chapter 1: Introduction - introduces the research topic, background, motivation, 
objectives, scope, and limitations. 
Chapter 2: Literature Review  - reviews existing studies on Robotic Mobile 
Fulfillment Systems, storage allocation methods, and identifying research gaps. 
Chapter 3: Methodology  - describes the research flowchart, data collection and 
analyzing processes, optimization techniques, and model development approach used 
in the study. 
Chapter 4:  Result and Discussion  - presents the performance evaluation of the 
model using metrics like pod retrieval frequency, space utilization, and pile -on 
efficiency, along with an analysis of findings. 
Chapter 5: Conclusion - summarizes the key findings,

contributions, and limitations, 
and suggests future research directions for further improvement. 
 

 
 
7 
CHAPTER 2  
LITERATURE REVIEW 
2.1 Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) 
Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems have emerged as a transformative innovation 
in warehouse logistics, particularly within the e-commerce sector. Unlike traditional 
picker-to-parts systems where workers walk through aisles to retrieve items  [23], 
RMFS follows a parts-to-picker approach, where autonomous mobile robots transport 
storage units called pods, directly to human -operated or automated picking stations  
[20]. Several studies have shown that incorporating RMFS into warehouse systems 
can significantly cut down picker travel time, making higher picking rates more 
doable compared to traditional warehouse setups  [24]. Today, RMFS is widely 
adopted by leading companies such as Alibaba, JD.com, and Cainiao,

Pods: Movable shelves that store various Stock Keeping Units [28]. Each pod 
consists of multiple compartments designed to hold items of different sizes 
and categories. A single pod may carry several types of products, depending 
on the SKU -to-pod assignment strategy adopted. When a pick or 
replenishment order is generated, the system identifies the appropriate pod(s) 
containing the required SKUs and instructs a robot to fetch them.  
2.

Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs): battery-powered robots that are capable 
of navigating warehouse environments using a grid of waypoints and pre -
defined paths  [29]. Each robot is equipped with a lifting mechanism that 
allows it to move underneath a pod, lift it, and carry it to the designated 
workstation [30]. Once the task is completed, the robot returns the pod to an 
optimized storage location based on inventory rules. Advanced RMFS 
platforms use intelligent robot scheduling and path planning algorithms to 
avoid congestion, prevent deadlocks, and minimize travel distances.

3. Workstations: Areas where workers perform pod -related tasks such as 
replenishment, picking, and packing [32]. There are two main types: picking 
stations and replenishment stations. At a picking station, workers retrieve 
items from the pod to fulfill customer orders. For replenishment, the robot 
delivers a pod to a replenishment station where new inventory is added to 
maintain stock levels.

When a customer order enters the system, it is first assigned to an available 
pick

ing station. To fulfill the order, AMRs are dispatched to retrieve pods that 
contain the required SKUs from the storage area. These pods are then delivered 
directly to the assigned picking station, allowing workers to efficiently pick the 
necessary items without navigating through wareh

ouse aisles  [28]. If the inventory 
level of a particular SKU within a pod falls below a predefined threshold, the system 
automatically schedules the pod for replenishment. In such cases, an AMR transports 
the pod to a replenishment station, where stock levels are restored before the pod is 
returned to storage or reused for future pic king tasks. This seamless flow between 
picking and replenishment enhances operational efficiency and supports continuous 
order processing. The workflow in an RMFS warehouse is depicted in Figure 2.3.

2.2 Decision Problem in RMFS 
Merschformann et al. [21] structured the decisions problems in RMFS into several 
levels of warehouse operation, with each influencing different aspects of system 
perf

ormance. These decisions are categorized into three main levels: strategic, tactical, 
and operational, each with distinct objectives and time periods.  
Strategic level decisions are made during the construction phase of the warehouse 
and have long -term

implications. At this stage, one of the key considerations is 
workstation placement, which involves deciding where to p osition picking and 
replenishment stations in relation to the storage area. Another important factor is 
storage area dimensioning, which includes determining the overall size of the storage 
space, how it’s divided into zones, and how aisles are arranged. 


 
 
10 
Tactical decisions in an RMFS are typically made at the beginning of an 
operational period, such as the start of a workweek, workday or shift. These decisions 
involve determining number of pods per SKU, ratio of pick and replenishment 
stations, replenishment

level, and resource reallocation. These tactical decisions help 
the system stay agile and responsive to short -term changes, ensuring smooth 
operations throughout the day or week. 
Operational-level decisions are executed in real time and directly govern the flow 
of robots, pods, and orders. These are the most dynamic and data-driven decisions in 
an RMFS and

are divided into four main components: 
1. Order Assignment (OA) decides which station will handle each incoming 
order. 
- Pick Order Assignment (POA): Assign s customer orders to picking 
stations. 
- Replenishment Order Assignment (ROA): Assigns replenishment tasks to 
available replenishment stations. 
2. Task Creation (TC) involves transforming those assigned orders into specific 
robot task

s. 
- Pick Pod Selection (PPS) : Choosing which pod to bring to fulfil picking 
orders. 
- Replenishment Pod Selection (RPS): Selecting a pod with available space 
to store incoming inventory 
- Pod Storage Assignment (PSA): Deciding where to place a pod after it 
completes a task, with goals of  minimizing future travel and balancing 
access. 
3. Task Allocation (TA) deciding where to place a pod after it completes a task, 
with goals of minimizing future travel and balancing access. 
4. Path Planning (PP) deciding where to place a pod after it completes a task, 
with goals of minimizing future travel and balancing access. 

 
 
11

Figure 2. 4 The Relations of Decision Problems in RMFS [21] 
Effectively addressing these decision problems through well -designed rules and 
coordinated strategies enables RMFS to operate with high efficiency and reliability. 
Integrated decision-making is essential to optimize throughput, reduce delays, and 
ensure timely and accurate order fulfilment, ultimately contributing to superior 
system performance and customer satisfaction. 
Storage Assignment Problem 
In Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems, the storage assignment problem is a 
critical decision problem in tactical level [21], significantly impacting the operational 
efficiency and throughput of fulfillment operations. It involves assigning SKUs to 
appropriate pods in a manner that enhances overall picking performance, reduces 
robot travel time, and impr

oves system responsiveness. Unlike traditional warehouses 
where items are placed in fixed racks and retrieved manually, RMFS involves 
dynamic inventory storage and retrieval facilitated by AMRs, making storage 
decisions more complex and interdependent with system-wide performance factors. 
A number of studies have investigated SAP in RMFS settings by focusing on SKU 
correlation and clust

For instance, Kim et al.  [33] 
and Li et al.  [34] explored storage assignment strategies that aim to maximize the 
correlation degree among SKUs, grouping frequently co-ordered items together in the 
same pods to reduce the number of pod visits per order. Mirzaei et al. [35] expanded 
on this approach by proposing an integrated cluster allocation policy that accounts for 
both item correlation and turnover rates. Similarl y, Yang  [36] emphasized item 
similarity as a basis for storage location assignment. However, all the 


 
 
12 
abovementioned studies operate under the constraint that each SKU is assigned to 
only one pod or group, a design that may limit flexibility and reduce opportunities for 
optimization under dynamic

order scenarios. 
In contrast, recent studies have introduced scattered storage as a more flexible and 
efficient strategy. In this model, a single SKU can be stored across multiple pods, 
thereby significantly enhance picking efficiency and is suitable for RMFS warehouse.  
Xiang et al. [37] using historical order data addressed the small -scale item storage 
assignment problem under the scattered storage method and solved it using a CPLEX 
solver. Mirzaei et al.

[22] proposed a correlated dispersion storage policy that 
integrates SKU correlation, turnover frequency, and dispersion across pods. This 
approach improves picking performance by ensuring that items with high co -
occurrence and turnover rates are accessible f rom multiple pods without excessive 
redundancy. Ma et al.

[10] added a classification layer, distinguishing between best-
selling and general -selling items. Their model incorporated item classification 
alongside correlation and scattered storage considera tions to devise a more 
comprehensive storage policy.  Zhang et al. [38] further enriched the literature by 
accounting for human factors. Their research considered picking efficiency and 
pickers’ physical effort.  A more detailed comparison of previous studies on the 
storage assignment problem is presented in Tab

le 2.1.  
However, despite the valuable contributions, a critical limitation remains across 
all the aforementioned studies: they did not explicitly consider the physical 
dimensions of SKU s and the size constraints of compartments within pods when 
assigning SKUs to storage locations. In real-world RMFS environments, each pod is 
divided into compartments with fixed geometrical capacities, and SKUs vary in

size 
and shape. Ignoring these crucial constraints can lead to theoretically optimal storage 
policies that are infeasible in practice. As such, solutions derived without considering 
volume fitting, dimension compatibility, and slot availability may not translate well 
into operational setting s. Bridging this gap requires integrating physical feasibility 
checks into the storage assignment logic to ensure that assigned SKUs can actually 
be placed in the intended c

ompartments.

2.3 Item Correlation 
Item correlation is widely recognized as a crucial concept in modern warehouse 
optimization and plays an important role in solving the storage assignment problem 
within Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems. As highlighted in the previou

s 
subchapter, many recent studies (see Table 2.1) use item correlation to improve SKU-
to-pod assignment strategies. When products with high correlation are placed together 
within the same pod, they are more likely to be appeared together in a single order  
[41]. This increases the number of items that can be retrieved in a single pod trip, 
thereby reducing the overall number of pod visits required to complete order 
fulfillment. 
By understanding how products are frequently ordered together, researchers and 
practitioners can identify meaningful relationships that helps clustering, scattered 
storage policies, and mixed storage configurations. This data-driven perspective he

lps 
RMFS warehouses boost picking efficiency, reduce unnecessary robot travel, and 
adapt dynamically to changing demand patterns. 
Market Basket Analysis is one of the most widely applied techniques for 
uncovering these item relationships  that associated with customer buying behaviour 
[42]. MBA relies on Association Rule Mining to analyze transaction data, detecting 
which items appear together in customer orders. Studies such as Ünvan  [43] 
demonstrate that robust algorithms like Apriori and FP-Growth can effectively reveal 
strong correlations between SKUs. These patterns can then be used to group ite ms 
with high co-occurrence, ultimately improving layout and storage allocation decisions 
in practice. 
The strategic impact of understanding item correlation extends beyond simple 
pairings. Kumar et al. [44] emphasize that recognizing hidden interdependencies 
among SKUs enables companies to design smarter replenishment rules and cross -
selling opportunitie s, while Sajwan and Tripathi [45] show that a comprehensive 
MBA framework can illuminate the more complex interdependencies in shopping 
baskets, allowing companies to tailor promotions, manage stock levels, and even 
redesign store layouts to reflect actu al buying behavior. Their study underscores the 
potential of ARM for generating strategic business insights, rather than treating it 
solely as a technical data mini

ng task. 
In the context of RMFS, association rules hold particular value for SKU -to-pod 
assignment and storage design. By identifying frequently co -ordered items, 

 
 
15 
warehouse managers can cluster highly correlated SKUs within the same pods or 
strategically disperse them to balance picking efficiency and travel distance. The 
insights gained through ARM thus align closely with broader warehouse optimization 
goals, including increase order

throughput and pile-on. 
 
2.4 SKU Classification 
Effective SKU classification is a fundamental component in optimizing storage 
strategies within RMFS. As modern warehouse s handle thousands of diverse SKUs 
with varying demand patterns, dimensions, and turnover rates, classifying SKUs 
appropriately enables more intelligent decisions in storage assignment, order picking, 
and replenishment planning. By grouping SKUs based on s hared attributes such as 
demand frequency, volatility, or co -occurrence in orders, RMFS can achieve higher 
picking efficiency and reduce unnecessary pod movements. 
Several studies have highlighted the importance of SKU classification in 
improving warehouse performance, such as reduce pick travel distance and enhance 
throughput.  Yuan et al. [46] investigated pod assignment strategies in RMFS by 
comparing random, class -based, and turnover -based approache

s. Their findings 
indicate that class -based storage u sing two or three categories captures most of the 
potential advantages , with having more variation . Muppani and Adil [13] 
demonstrated that implementing a class -based storage policy, which groups SKUs 
with similar demand patterns, can reduce both order picking distance and required 
storage space. This approach improves space utilization and helps ensure that high -
turnover products are stored in more accessible locations. Another study by Mir

zaei 
et al. [22] demonstrated that combining turnover -based, correlation -based, and 
dispersed storage strategies in robotic warehouses can lead to significant cost savings 
and reduce oper ational time. By aligning SKU placement with both demand 
frequency and product correlations, and distributing inventory across multiple pods, 
warehouses can reduce retrieval effort, limit pod congestion, and improve order 
fulf

illment efficiency. This integ rated approach minimizes unnecessary robot travel 
and redundant handling, resulting in lower operational costs. 
 
2.5 Coefficient of Variation (CV) 
In order to effectively manage inventory and improve storage assignment in 
Robotic Mobile Fulfilment Systems, it is important to recognize that not all SKUs 

 
 
16 
b

ehave the same in terms of demand. One way to capture this variability is through 
the Coefficient of Variation, a statistical measure that measures the ratio of the 
standard deviation to the mean of a data set [47]. The coefficient of variation is widely 
used across various fields such as agriculture, industry, healthcare, social sciences, 
and education, serving multipl

e research and analytical purposes [48]. In the context 
of inventory and warehouse manageme nt, CV serves as a normalized indicator to 
quantify the volatility of SKU demand, providing a solid foundation for setting 
differentiated inventory and storage allocation policy. 
In an RMFS environment, where storage space is mobile and dynamic, 
understanding the volatility of SKU demand is essential for optimizing SKU -to-pod 
assignments. This study applies CV as a decision variable to determine the inventory 
servi

ce level and the dispersion strategy for each SKU. SKUs with low CV values 
typically show stab le and predictable demand. In contrast, SKUs with higher CV 
va

lues tend to display irregular or intermittent demand.  
The relevance of using CV for classification has been well -established across 
various fields. For example, the ABC -XYZ classification fram ework, which 
combines demand quantity (ABC) with demand variability (XYZ), applies the CV as 
a core measure to distinguish between stable (X class), moderately variable (Y-class), 
and highly erratic (Z class) items [49]. This combined approach helps companies align 
their inventory control and replenishment strategies with the real patterns of order. 
Beyond warehousing, the value of CV as a simple yet robust analytical tools has 
also been demonstrated in fields such as agricultural monitoring, where it was used to 
classify cropland ar

eas across diverse climates based on temporal backscatter data  
[50]. Taken together, these examples highlight that integrating CV into SKU 
analytical tools enables more responsive, data-driven storage and inventory decisions, 
ultimately supporting higher picking efficiency and system throughput in RMFS 
operations.

3.5 Application of Dimensional Constraints 
In this study, dimensional constraints are explicitly applied to ensure that the SKU 
to compartmen

t assignment is physically feasible and operationally realistic. In real 
RMFS operations, pods are divided into compartments with fixed geometrical 
capacities, and SKUs vary significantly in their dimensions and packaging shapes. By 
applying dimensional constraints during the storage assignment process, the system 
aims to achieve more compact SKU allocation, thereby minimizing the total number 
of pods required to satisfy inventory requirements. 
A structured procedure is implemented to identify the optimal placement of SKUs 
and to determine the most suitable pod and compartment type, while ensuring that 
dimensional constraints are satisfied: 
1. Dimension sorting: each SKU’s and compartment’s dimensions are sorted into 
longest, second longest, and shortest sides. This standardization ensures 
consistent orientation when evaluating fit within compartments. 
2.

Fit assessment: for each SKU, the fit is checked across five predefined types  of 
compartments. The maximum quantity that can be stored within each 
compartment type is then calculated based on the SKU’s physical dimensions and 
the capacity of the compartment. 
3.

Maximum capacity and inventory level comparison: the calculated maximum 
capacity for each SKU to compartment pair is compared with the SKU’s minimum 
required inventory level. This comparison ensures that the selected storage

configuration can support operational replenishment needs without exceeding 
compartment constraints.

3.6 SKU Dispersion 
Scattered or dispersed storage has been widely recognized in the literature as an 
effective approach to enhance picking efficiency in warehouse systems. By 
distributing SKUs across multiple storage locations or pods, the likelihood of having 
required items closer to pick stations increases, thereby reducing travel distances and 
improving system responsiveness. As summarized in Chapter 2, Table 2.1, several 
studies have demonstrated that this strategy is especially beneficial in RMFS 
warehouse. 

 
 
26 
In this study, SKU dispersion is implemente

d based on the CV  of each SKU’s 
order frequency. The CV serves as a normalized measure of demand variability, 
offering a standardized basis for determining how widely each SKU should be 
distributed within the storage system. A higher CV indicates greater variability and 
unpredictability in order patterns, wh

SKUs with high CV values are dispersed across a larger number of pods. This 
strategy increases the chances that SKUs whose demand patterns are sporadic, are 
available when needed, improving the system’s responsiveness and reducing the risk 
of pod unavailability. In contrast, SKUs with low CV values exhibit stable and 
predictable demand and are therefore assigned to fewer pods to save storage space 
and avoids storing the same items unneces

sarily.

CHAPTER 5  
CONCLUSION 
5.1 Conclusion 
The rapid rise of Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems has introduced numerous 
decision-making challenges aimed at

improving order fulfillment efficiency. Among 
these, the optimization of the Storage Assignment Problem (SAP) has emerged as a 
critical concern , as it directly affects warehouse performance . While a variety of 
performance metrics have been proposed to evaluate SKU allocation strategies, 
maximizing throughput remains

the main goal across all warehouse operations. Most 
research on SAP has primarily  emphasized SKU correlation and item classification 
methods, which are indeed important for formulating allocation policies. However, 
such approaches often assume the presence of strong SKU relationship

s, which is not 
always the case in every warehouse . However, despite the valuable contributions, a 
critical research gap  remains across previous studies, they did not consider the 
physical dimensions of SKUs and the size constraints of compartments within pods 
when assigning SKUs to storage locations. 
In this study, we proposed a SKU-to-pod allocation strategy specifically designed 
for RMFS warehouses, incorporating several key operational factors such as order 
frequency, product turnover rates, product correlation, and dimensional constraints.  
Our approach be gan with a systematic analysis of historical orde

r data to classify 
SKUs based on demand  frequency and stability . We proposed two SKU -to-pod 
policies based on  this SKUs classification: one pod one class policy and one pod 
mixed class policy, we then compared these two allocation policies with the random 
policy baseline. To evaluate the real -world applicability of the proposed policies, 
dimensional constraints were applied to all three scenarios. Furthermore, a dispersion 
policy was introduced to inves tigate the effect of distributing SKUs across multiple 
pods, thereby increasing SKU accessibility.  A simulation model was developed using 
NetLogo for agent -based modeling and Python for optimization, and performance 
was evaluated using a dataset of 7,024 SKUs. 
Simulation results showed that under dimensional constraints alone, the One Pod 
Mixed Class strategy achieved the best performance, with mean throughput of 
91.90% and mean pile‑on of 9.79, followed by One Pod One Class with mean 
throughput of 85.23% and mean pile‑on of 9.37 and the Random baseline with mean 

 
 
53 
throughput of 78.65% and mean pile‑on of 7.35. After applying the dispersion policy, 
both metrics improved across all scenarios. Mix Class scenario throughput increased 
to 99.22%, One Class scenario to 96.22%, and Random scenario to  79.93%, while 
pile‑on rose to 9.96, 9.75, and 7.46, respectively.  These gains coincide with greater 
storage usage fr

om roughly 273 to 472 pods, reflecting the trade‑off between 
dispersion and pod capacity requirements. 
Hypothesis testing using ANOVA confirmed significant differences in both 
throughput and pile-on across all scenarios under dimensional constraints and under 
the dispersion policy (p < 0.05). Paired t-tests further verified that introducing the 
dispersion policy led to stat

istically significant improvements in throughput and pile-
on for all three allocation strategies (p < 0.05).  These results validate the effectiveness 
of SKU classification by demand frequency and stability, and demonstrate that 
dispersion can further enhance performance by increasing SKU accessibility, while 
also highlighting the importance of considering physical storage constraints in real -
world RMFS optimization. 
 
5.2 Recommendation and Future Research 
While this study has successfully proposed a structured SKU -to-pod allocation 
policy and demonstrated significant improvements in both throughput and pile -on 
performance, several directions remain open for future exploration to further enhance 
RMFS operations. 
The current classification framework is primarily based on demand stability . 
However, additional factors such as SKU physical size, weight, and handling 
complexity could be integrated into the classification criteria to create more 
comprehensive storage profi

les.  
Future research could incorporate pod weight balancing when designing SKU-to-
pod assignment policies. Uneven weight distribution within pods may increase the 
mechanical burden on AMRs and negatively affect battery efficiency  and energy 
consumption. Introducing a weighted balancing constraint during SKU assignment 
could help ensure pod stability and improve the energy consump

tion of AMRs. 
Continuing from previous recommendation, energy consumption is an 
increasingly important performance metric in modern warehouse systems. While this 
study focused on throughput and pile-on, future work could evaluate strategies based 

 
 
54 
on energy efficiency, such as minimizing total robot travel distance or power usage , 
thus aligning system design

with sustainability goals. 
Finally, a promising area lies in developing dynamic SKU -to-pod policies that 
adapt in real time to changing demand patterns. As product lifecycles shorten and 
customer preferences fluctuate , integrating predictive analytics or reinforcement 
learning, future systems can reconfigure SKU placements periodically, ensuring 
sustained efficiency under

evolving operational conditions. 
In summary, while this research has laid a solid foundation for a feasible and 
effective SKU allocation policy in RMFS, future enhancements that incorporate 
product-specific attributes, energy efficiency metrics, and real-time adaptability hold 
great potential to advance the state-of-the-art in intelligent warehouse automation. By 
building upon the groundwork established in this study and exploring the proposed 
extensions, future research can further drive the e volution of RMFS and contribute 
meaningfully to broader innovations in automated warehouse management.
